1: A revelation of God: “Woe to those who devise wickedness and work evil on their beds! When the morning dawns, they carry it out, because it is in their power.” The God who reveals this woe is the God who sees what is devised on beds in the night — the omniscient judge who cannot be evaded. Christ's omniscience is expressed similarly: he “knew what was in a man” (John 2:25) and reads the intentions of the heart without being told (Mark 2:8). The woe against those who “plan iniquity” is the OT form of the NT's “you have heard it was said... but I say to you that everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery” (Matthew 5:28) — judgment reaches the heart, not just the act.
2: A revelation of God: the seizure of fields and houses — “they covet fields and seize them; they oppress a man and his house, a man and his inheritance” — violates the Torah's land-inheritance laws (Lev 25; Numbers 36:7-9) which protected each family's permanent claim to the land YHWH gave them. The Naboth incident (1 Kgs 21) is the paradigmatic case. The God who condemns the seizure of the poor man's inheritance is the God whose Son declares “Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth” (Matthew 5:5) — the land-inheritance promise is transformed into the new creation inheritance that cannot be seized.
3: A shadow: “Therefore, thus says the LORD: I am devising disaster against this family.” YHWH's counter-planning against those who plan evil (v1: “devise iniquity”) — a divine measure-for-measure response. This reveals a God who acts in history with moral precision, not passively. The NT's form: “Do not be deceived: God is not mocked, for whatever one sows, that will he also reap” (Galatians 6:7). But the cross introduces an asymmetry into the moral calculus: Christ takes the disaster that was devised against us (Isaiah 53:10: “it was the will of the LORD to crush him”) so that those who repent escape the disaster they have devised.
4: A shadow: “We are utterly ruined; he changes the portion of my people.” The lament that will be sung over the oppressors who become the oppressed — the reversal that judgment brings. The Magnificat enacts this same reversal at the coming of Christ: “he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate” (Luke 1:52). The bitter taunt-song over those who seized others' inheritances is the OT form of Christ's “Woe to you who are rich” (Luke 6:24) — the reversal of fortune in the coming kingdom is structurally identical.
5: A shadow: “Therefore you will have no one to cast the line by lot in the assembly of the LORD.” Loss of the right to allot land in the community — permanent exclusion from the covenant inheritance. The NT's warning has the same structure: “Whoever has, more will be given; but whoever does not have, even what he has will be taken from him” (Mark 4:25). Those who seize what is not theirs lose what they have; those who receive in faith inherit everything. Christ is the one in whom all the inheritance-promises are “Yes and Amen” (2 Corinthians 1:20).
6: A theme: the command to silence the prophets — “Do not preach; one should not preach of such things” — is the persistent attempt to suppress divine judgment before it reaches its audience. The same silencing is attempted against Christ (Mark 10:48: the crowd telling Bartimaeus to be quiet) and the apostles (Acts 4:17-18: “commanded them not to speak... in the name of Jesus”). The word of God cannot be ultimately silenced: “Is not my word like fire, declares the LORD?” (Jeremiah 23:29). What was said of Christ is said of all faithful witnesses who proclaim judgment.
7: A revelation of God: “Should this be said, O house of Jacob? Is the Spirit of the LORD impatient? Are these his deeds?” — the people question whether YHWH could really act this harshly. YHWH's response: “Do not my words do good to him who walks uprightly?” The Spirit of the LORD is not impatient but consistent: the word that judges the oppressor does good to the upright. Christ makes the same point about the Spirit's work: the Spirit “will convict the world concerning sin and righteousness and judgment” (John 16:8) — the same word that judges the guilty vindicates the righteous.
8: A shadow: “But lately my people have risen up as an enemy — you strip the rich robe from those who pass by trustingly.” Attacking the peaceable, stripping the garment of the trusting — covenant betrayal at its most intimate. At the crucifixion, the soldiers strip Christ's garments (John 19:23-24, fulfilling Psalms 22:18) — the faithful and trusting one is stripped by those who should have been his people. The covenant violation that Micah describes reaches its nadir at the cross, where the covenant people hand over the covenant Lord to be stripped and crucified.
9: A shadow: “The women of my people you drive out from their pleasant houses; from their young children you take away my splendor forever.” The expulsion of the vulnerable — women and children driven from homes — violates the deepest covenantal obligations of neighbor-care. Christ's specific concern for the vulnerable (Matthew 18:1-5; Micah 19:13-15: “let the little children come to me”; Luke 18:3 the persistent widow) is the positive form of Micah's indictment. The splendor taken from the children is restored in the new creation: “the kingdom of God belongs to such as these” (Mark 10:14).
10: A shadow: “Arise and go, for this is no place to rest, because of uncleanness that destroys with a grievous destruction.” Exile as the consequence of defiling the land — the land vomits out its inhabitants when they defile it (Leviticus 18:25). The pattern of exile and rest: Israel has no rest in the defiled land; Christ promises “rest for your souls” (Matthew 11:29) in the new covenant. The true rest is not a geographic location but a person: “there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God” (Hebrews 4:9) that the exile forfeited and Christ restores.
11: A theme: the false prophet who would prophesy wine and strong drink — “if a man of wind and falsehood comes and preaches lies” — exposes the market for comfortable prophecy: people prefer preachers who tell them what they want to hear (cf. 2 Timothy 4:3-4: “having itching ears they will accumulate for themselves teachers to suit their own passions”). Christ's contrast is absolute: “I am the truth” (John 14:6) — not the comforting lie but the demanding and liberating truth. The test of a true prophet (his words come true) is the test the resurrection applies definitively to Christ.
12: A type: “I will surely assemble all of you, O Jacob; I will gather the remnant of Israel; I will set them together like sheep in a fold, like a flock in its pasture.” The gathering Shepherd collecting the scattered remnant — this is the explicit type of the Good Shepherd. Christ quotes Ezek 34 (the same tradition) and applies it to himself: “I have other sheep that are not of this fold; I must bring them also” (John 10:16). The remnant-gathering promise of Micah 2:12 is fulfilled in Christ's mission: “the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost” (Luke 19:10), gathering the scattered flock.
13: A type: “He who opens the breach goes up before them; they break through and pass the gate, going out by it. Their king passes on before them, the LORD at their head.” The poretz (breaker, the one who breaks through) leading the flock out through the breach — an image of the liberating king who goes ahead of his people, smashing the walls that confine them. This is one of the richest messianic types in Micah: Christ as the one who breaks through death (Acts 2:24: “God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it”) and leads the redeemed out from death's enclosure. John 10:3-4 applies the same shepherd-leader pattern to Christ: he “goes before them, and the sheep follow him.”
